    Sprint 0 – Problem Framing, Infrastructure Setup & RAG Foundations

    📄

    What is RAG?

    Lesson 3

    Introduction


    Large Language Models (LLMs) have greatly improved natural language processing, enabling fluent and coherent text generation. However, they still face challenges such as limited and static knowledge from their training data and the tendency to generate inaccurate or fabricated facts. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) addresses these issues by combining LLMs with external knowledge sources during the generation process.


    In a RAG system, when a query is made, relevant information is retrieved from an external database or set of documents. This retrieved information is then provided to the LLM as additional context. By doing so, the LLM can generate more accurate, current, and contextually relevant responses. Essentially, RAG allows models to access updated information and domain-specific knowledge beyond their initial training data.


    This lesson will outline the key components of RAG, the motivations behind its use, how information flows within RAG systems, important design considerations, suitable use cases, and the current trade-offs associated with implementing RAG effectively.



    Key Conceptual Components of RAG


    At a high level, modern RAG systems can be understood as consisting of several conceptual components that ensure information is retrieved, aligned, and injected into the model's context in a usable form. The key components include:


    • Retrieval Abstraction: The retriever provides an abstraction over external knowledge, selecting content at an appropriate granularity and semantic level for the query. Rather than treating the knowledge base as a monolithic text blob, RAG frameworks chunk and index data into semantically meaningful pieces (e.g. passages, sections, facts) and retrieve only what's relevant. This abstraction can operate at multiple levels - for example, retrieving a whole document vs. a specific paragraph - depending on the query's needs. Choosing the right level of abstraction is crucial - too fine-grained retrieval might require many pieces and risk the "needle in a haystack" effect (important info drowned out by volume), whereas too coarse retrieval might include irrelevant or redundant text as entire passages would be passed to the LLM's context.

    • Factual Grounding: A primary purpose of RAG is to ground the generative model's output in retrieved evidence. By explicitly conditioning on verifiable text, the model is less likely to hallucinate unsupported content and more likely to produce answers consistent with known facts. In a well-designed RAG system, every non-trivial fact in the answer ideally traces back to something in the retrieved context. This grounding not only improves factual accuracy but also provides transparency - downstream users or systems can inspect the sources that the model used (this is why RAG is popular in applications requiring evidence or citations). Grounding does assume that the external knowledge contains the truth. If the corpus is outdated or wrong, the model will faithfully repeat those errors. Thus, RAG shifts the responsibility of completeness and correctness to the retrieval corpus.

    • Representation Alignment: For RAG systems to work well, the way the retriever finds information and the way the generator (LLM) uses that information must match closely. Representation alignment involves making sure that the query and documents retrieved match in meaning and retrieved documents provide exactly what the LLM needs to answer effectively. If the retriever returns information that's related but doesn't directly help answer the question, the LLM might produce poor answers. There are techniques that help improve this alignment, e.g. query reformulation - changing or expanding the original question to better match document content or embedding fine-tuning - adjusting embeddings so queries and documents are closer in meaning. In short, representation alignment ensures that the right information is retrieved and is in a format the LLM can easily use.

    • Dynamic Context Injection: Retrieval-augmented generation is fundamentally about dynamically injecting external context into the model's input at query time. Unlike static prompts or fine-tuning, RAG treats the retrieved evidence as a variable context that is determined on the fly for each query. This dynamic injection means the system can flexibly pull in different data depending on the user's request, making the model's responses highly situational and context-sensitive. Effective context injection requires attention to how much and how the info is inserted, e.g. prefixing the user query with an instruction like "Here are some relevant excerpts:" followed by the text. One benefit of dynamic context injection is adaptability: the same model can serve many domains - the retrieved content determines the domain knowledge used for that instance. Another benefit is precision: only information estimated to be relevant is added, which optimizes the use of the limited context space. Notably, dynamic context injection is what allows RAG systems to remain up-to-date without retraining: if new information becomes available, it can be retrieved and injected at inference time, immediately influencing answers.


    These components work together. The retriever abstracts and selects knowledge, the grounding ensures the model sticks to that evidence, alignment makes the retrieval and generation speak the same language, and dynamic injection is the mechanism tying it together into the model's input.



    The scheme of naive RAG



    Retrieval Components



    F) Chunking


    What It Is: Breaking down external knowledge into smaller, manageable pieces to retrieve and use effectively.


    Best Practices:

    • Chunk Size: Balance between small (precise but possibly incomplete context) and large chunks (comprehensive but risk context dilution).

    • Windowing: Use sliding (overlapping) or tumbling (non-overlapping) window strategies depending on context continuity requirements.

    • Parent-Child Retrieval: Consider retrieving related or hierarchical chunks alongside primary results to enrich context.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Overly small chunks losing meaningful context.

    • Overly large chunks overwhelming model attention and reducing relevance.



    C) Embedding Model Selection


    What It Is: Choosing appropriate embedding models for creating vector representations of queries and contexts.


    Best Practices:

    • Semantic Relevance: Select embedding models aligned with your domain (e.g., SentenceTransformers, OpenAI embeddings).

    • Contextual Embeddings: Employ contextual embedding models when context sensitivity is crucial.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Using embedding models poorly aligned with the domain, reducing retrieval accuracy.

    • Neglecting contextual embeddings, limiting semantic understanding.



    D) Vector Database


    What It Is: Storing and managing vector representations efficiently to support rapid retrieval.


    Best Practices:

    • Database Choice: Use well-supported vector databases (e.g., Weaviate, Pinecone, Qdrant) suited to your scale and performance needs.

    • Hosting: Choose appropriate hosting solutions (cloud-managed services for scalability vs. self-hosted for security).

    • Metadata Management: Store meaningful metadata (timestamps, document IDs, sources) to facilitate effective pre- and post-filtering.

    • Indexing Strategy: Optimize indexing for query performance, balancing accuracy and retrieval speed.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Choosing an unsuitable database causing performance issues.

    • Neglecting metadata management, preventing efficient retrieval.



    E) Vector Search


    What It Is: Retrieving relevant information based on semantic similarity.


    Best Practices:

    • Similarity Measures: Select suitable similarity metrics (cosine similarity, dot product, Euclidean distance) based on data type and use case.

    • Query Path: Determine whether to prioritise metadata filtering (metadata-first) or semantic retrieval (ANN-first) based on query characteristics.

    • Hybrid Search: Combine semantic and lexical search methods to optimise accuracy.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Using inappropriate similarity measures, reducing retrieval accuracy.

    • Overlooking hybrid search methods, missing opportunities for improved accuracy.



    G) Retrieval Heuristics


    What It Is: Applying specific business rules or conditions to guide retrieval.


    Best Practices:

    • Time Importance: Prioritise recent or relevant time-specific information.

    • Diversity Ranking: Ensure diverse results to prevent redundant context.

    • Source Verification: Emphasise credible or authoritative sources in retrieval.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Ignoring heuristics leading to irrelevant or outdated context.

    • Insufficient diversity leading to repetitive context.



    Key Generation Components



    A) Choosing the Large Language Model (LLM)


    What It Is: Selecting the most suitable generative model to produce accurate, context-sensitive responses.


    Best Practices:

    • Evaluate models based on domain expertise, generation accuracy, latency, and inference cost.

    • Choose models with the appropriate context length for the task.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Selecting models without sufficient domain understanding or context length, harming accuracy.



    B) Prompt Engineering


    What It Is: Crafting effective prompts to guide the model's responses.


    Best Practices:

    • Clearly specify desired response formats.

    • Include explicit instructions on how to integrate retrieved information.

    • Use techniques to prevent jailbreak scenarios and maintain model safety.

    Common Mistakes:

    • Assuming retrieved context eliminates the need for careful prompt crafting.

    • Ignoring security implications, leading to potential misuse.



    Feedback Loops

    • Continuously refine chunking, embeddings, retrieval heuristics, and prompts based on monitoring insights.

    • Regularly revisit retrieval and generation strategies to ensure alignment and factual grounding.



    Architectural Design Patterns in RAG Systems


    • Modular Composition of Components: RAG architectures are inherently modular - the retriever and generator are separate modules with a clear interface (the retrieved text). This separation of concerns means each module can be improved or scaled independently (you can swap out the vector database or retriever model without retraining the generator, etc.). In fact, modern RAG pipelines often include additional modules beyond just retrieve-and-generate. There may be a search module (for keyword search), a reranker module (to refine the retrieval results), a fusion module (to merge info from multiple documents), or a task-specific adapter module (to handle formatting or decision routing). The motivation here is maintainability and flexibility - if the knowledge source changes (say from a document corpus to a database), you only replace or augment the retrieval module. If you want to add a step (like an intermediate summarisation of retrieved texts), you can insert a module for that. For AI engineers, thinking in terms of modules (retriever, ranker, generator, etc.) is crucial to design scalable RAG systems, as each module can be optimised (e.g., caching retrieval results, or fine-tuning the retriever separately).

    • Separation of Parametric Memory and External Memory: One of the most powerful architectural principles in RAG is the separation of the model's internal knowledge from external knowledge sources. By design, a RAG system keeps most world knowledge in an external knowledge base (vector store, search index, etc.) rather than relying on the LLM's fixed parameters. The LLM thus acts more as a reasoner and composer of information, while the external source acts as the storage of facts. This is analogous to how a computers CPU uses RAM disk - the model doesn't "carry" all data in its weights, it retrieves data from an external store when needed. Architecturally, this has multiple benefits. It makes the system updatable and adaptable - new data can be added to the knowledge store continually without touching the LLM (which is especially important for applications that need real-time knowledge). It also can be more efficient and cost-effective - instead of retraining or fine-tuning a giant model for every new dataset, you can simply index the data and let the model retrieve it. Another benefit is security and domain control - the external memory can be domain-scoped or access-controlled (e.g. a company's private documents), and the model will only output what it retrieves from that controlled set. Many enterprise LLM deployments have adopted this pattern for governance reasons.

    • Adaptive Context Shaping: Given the fixed capacity of the LLM's input context, RAG systems must use strategies to engineer the context that is fed into the model. This design pattern involves any logic that filters, orders, or transforms retrieved information to maximise its usefulness to the LLM. For example, if 10 documents are retrieved but only, say, 3 can fit well in context, the system might adaptively select the best subset (using a secondary relevance heuristic). This could be as simple as taking the top-k scoring passages. Another aspect of context shaping is compression: sometimes instead of dropping useful info, the system might summarise retrieved text to fit more content into the window. We also see context engineering used as a tool here - e.g., instructing the model to ignore certain parts or to focus on specific pieces, thereby shaping attention. An example technique is reranking or reordering the retrieved passages so that the most crucial facts appear at the beginning of the context (where, in some LLM architectures, they might receive more attention due to positional biases). Another technique is inserting headings or questions before each retrieved chunk to explicitly cue the model why that text is included (improving utilisation of context). In dynamic systems, context shaping could be query-dependent, e.g. for a definitional question you might include a short definition from a knowledge base plus a longer explanatory passage, whereas for a numerical question you might include a table or list of numbers and nothing else. Essentially, the pattern is that raw retrieval is followed by a refinement stage before generation. Architecturally, this may be implemented in the retrieval module (post-processing of results) or as its own step. From a conceptual standpoint, adaptive context shaping acknowledges that the retrieval -> LLM interface is bandwidth-limited and noisy, so it cleans and optimises that interface. By doing so, it directly improves answer quality and helps control things like hallucination (irrelevant text removed means the model is less likely to get distracted).


    Beyond these, there are other notable patterns (like iterative retrieval and generation loops, where the model can trigger further retrievals in a multi-turn approach, effectively breaking a complex query into multiple retrieve-read cycles). However, those can be seen as extensions of the above patterns, e.g., iterative RAG still relies on modular components, and it uses the generator output to adapt the next retrieval (another form of adaptive strategy). For most engineers, the above three patterns - modular architecture, memory separation, and context shaping - serve as guiding principles when designing a RAG system from scratch. They ensure the system is maintainable, scalable, and conceptually sound (each part does its job with minimal entanglement).



    When RAG Is (not) the Right Approach


    Not every problem calls for retrieval augmentation. It's important to understand the scenarios where RAG provides a clear benefit versus cases where it may be unnecessary or even counterproductive.



    Appropriate Scenarios for RAG:


    • Knowledge-Intensive Queries with Evolving Data: RAG shines in situations where the queries require up-to-date factual knowledge or reference to a broad knowledge base that changes over time. For example, open-domain question answering (like answering questions about current events, recent scientific discoveries, or a company's latest policies) is a textbook use case for RAG. Without retrieval, a frozen LLM would either not know the answer or might hallucinate. With retrieval, it can pull the answer from current data sources. In enterprise settings, any application that needs to draw on a large and dynamic document repository - such as customer support bots pulling answers from product manuals, or research assistants retrieving scholarly articles - is well-suited to RAG. The general rule is: if your task is "knowledge-intensive" (the solution involves substantial factual lookup) and the knowledge cannot be fully pre-encoded into the model, RAG is likely the appropriate approach.

    • Domain-Specific or Custom Corpora Requirements: If you need the model to operate over a specific set of documents (e.g., a law firm's private legal brief library, or a medical knowledge base) without leaking or missing domain details, RAG is appropriate. Instead of attempting to fine-tune the LLM on that entire corpus (which might be infeasible due to size or privacy), you use retrieval to ground answers in that corpus on demand. This ensures domain correctness - the model's outputs can be traced back to the official sources. For instance, an LLM supporting doctors should cite the hospital's internal guidelines or latest research for a treatment query. RAG makes this possible by retrieving those guidelines at query time. Essentially, whenever you have a curated knowledge source that should be the basis of truth, RAG is a way to enforce that the model uses it.

    • High Accountability and Need for Evidence: In applications where the traceability of answers is important (for trust or regulatory reasons), RAG is often a better approach than an end-to-end model. Because RAG can output not just an answer but also the supporting passages (the evidence), it facilitates human verification and builds trust. For example, a RAG-based assistant can say "According to document X, ... (quote) ... hence the answer is Y". Such an answer is far more verifiable than a raw LLM answer. If your use case requires that level of accountability - say, an AI assistant for researchers that must cite sources, or an AI for customer support where each answer should point to a knowledge base article - RAG is conceptually the right tool. Many AI architects choose RAG for this reason alone: it's easier to convince stakeholders of a system that quotes sources than one that mysteriously generates an answer.

    • Sparse Training Data for Task, but Lots of Unlabeled Text: If you're tackling a task where labeled data is scarce but you have access to lots of unstructured text relevant to the task, RAG can be a more practical solution than training a custom model. This pattern is common in virtual assistants or chatbot scenarios. Rather than training a model to internalize all information about a product or policy (which would require curated Q&A pairs or fine-tuning data), you can rely on retrieval from product documents. Here, RAG serves as a way to leverage raw text directly. The assumption is that "the answer is somewhere in these documents" - if that holds, RAG will find and present it. This scenario is essentially bootstrapping capabilities from text data without heavy training.

    • Multi-step Reasoning with External Tools: Some problem settings (like complex analytical queries) benefit from an agentic approach - the AI might plan a multi-step solution, use tools, retrieve information iteratively, etc. RAG is a natural fit for any such pipeline where one of the tools is information retrieval. If your system requires the AI to "research" something as part of its reasoning (e.g., a tutoring system that looks up reference material to answer a student's question), then architecturally you're looking at RAG or its generalization. The conceptual appropriateness lies in delegating knowledge lookup to an explicit step. So whenever the task is beyond the scope of one pass of reasoning and requires consulting external knowledge in stages, RAG (potentially extended to multiple turns) is likely the way to go.


    In short, RAG is appropriate when knowledge is a first-class citizen in the task - i.e., when success relies on accessing correct information that is not guaranteed to be already in the model. RAG is great in open-domain QA, enterprise knowledge assistants, search engines with narrative answers, personalized or context-aware assistants (that need to recall user-specific info or preferences), and any case where being grounded in reality is more important than generating purely novel text.



    When RAG May Not Be Needed (or Beneficial):


    • Tasks Requiring No External Knowledge: If the task is something like pure text transformation (summarization, translation, style transfer) or creative generation (story writing, open-ended brainstorming) where all required information is already contained in the input or is generic, RAG doesn't really apply. Injecting retrieved text in such cases might even confuse the model or constrain creativity unnecessarily. For example, if you ask an LLM to write a poem about the sunset, retrieving a Wikipedia article about sunsets isn't helpful - the model already has enough concept of "sunset" and the task doesn't call for factual grounding. So one boundary is - if the problem is not knowledge-intensive, adding retrieval adds complexity with no gain. Engineers often first analyze whether a given query set even benefits from outside info. If not, a plain LLM or a fine-tuned model on the specific task will be simpler and faster.

    • Stable Domains Where Fine-Tuning Suffices: If you're working in a narrow, static domain where the knowledge doesn't change much, an alternative to RAG is to simply fine-tune or prompt-engineer the model on that domain's data. For instance, if you have a fixed set of 100 product descriptions and you need the model to answer questions about them, you might encode that knowledge via fine-tuning or even by providing the descriptions in the prompt (if small enough). RAG is overkill when the knowledge base is small and static enough that it can be directly internalized by the model.

    • Extreme Latency or Simplicity Requirements: RAG pipelines are inherently more complex and typically slower than using a single LLM call. If you have a use case that demands ultra-low latency responses (e.g., a real-time system responding in a few milliseconds, or an embedded device with limited resources), the overhead of performing retrieval (especially vector search) and handling additional context might be too high. Also, every additional component (like a vector database) introduces points of failure and engineering complexity - if a simpler approach (even if slightly less accurate) is sufficient for the product, it might be chosen over a complex RAG system. Essentially, if speed, resource footprint, or system simplicity are top priorities and the task can be achieved "well enough" without retrieval, then RAG might not be worth the trade-off.

    • Incomplete or Unreliable Knowledge Source: RAG's effectiveness is bounded by the quality of the external knowledge source. If the knowledge base is missing critical information or contains a lot of noise, RAG can actually hurt performance. In cases where we cannot curate a decent corpus, relying on the model's own parametric knowledge (if it was trained on a broad corpus) might be safer. For instance, if one attempts RAG but the retrieved passages are often irrelevant or incorrect, the model could be led astray (garbage in, garbage out). In such a situation, focusing on improving retrieval or not using it until it's reliable is recommended. A well-known issue is that an LLM might actually have a correct fact in its parameters, but a poor retrieval returns a misleading passage, causing the model to produce a worse answer than it would have without RAG. Thus, when the retriever cannot be trusted, RAG is the wrong approach. It may sound obvious, but it's a reminder that RAG outsources knowledge to an external system - if that system is inferior, overall performance will degrade.

    • When Creativity and Coherence Trump Factuality: In tasks like long-form creative writing or complex narrative generation, injecting raw facts can disrupt the coherence or style of the output. If the primary goal is a coherent, stylistically consistent piece (and exact factual correctness is secondary), you might want to rely on the model's learned representation of the world rather than interrupting the generation with factual snippets. For instance, writing a fictional story or a philosophical essay likely doesn't need RAG - the model's internal "knowledge" and writing ability suffice.


    In summary, RAG is not a silver bullet for all LLM tasks. It specifically addresses the need for external knowledge and factual grounding. If your task doesn't have that need, or if introducing external knowledge is impractical, then RAG might be unnecessary. Engineers should weigh the complexity of RAG against alternatives like fine-tuning, bigger context models, or just using base models, based on the nature of the problem.



    Current Design Tensions and Challenges in RAG


    While RAG enables powerful capabilities, it also introduces new trade-offs and open challenges.


    • Latency vs. Factual Grounding: Every retrieval operation has a cost - making a query to a vector database or search engine adds latency to the pipeline. If a user question can be answered adequately from the LLM's memory, skipping retrieval could produce a faster response, whereas enabling retrieval will usually give a more accurate (grounded) answer but at the cost of extra milliseconds. Techniques like caching frequent retrieval results or doing asynchronous retrieval can help, but generally, grounding the answer in context takes time. In interactive settings (like chatbots), even a 1-second delay can impact user experience. Some systems make dynamic decisions via complexity routing - if a question looks fact-based, use RAG (slower but correct answer), if it looks opinion-based or trivial, answer directly for speed. Additionally, complex queries that might require multi-hop retrieval amplify this issue – doing two or three sequential retrievals and LLM calls will further increase latency, even if the end answer is very well-grounded. There's active research on minimising this overhead. For instance, efficient retrievers and indexing methods are being developed to cut down retrieval time.

    • Context Bottleneck and "Lost-in-the-Middle" Effect: Even though RAG strategically limits what goes into the prompt, the context window is still a hard bottleneck. If a query is broad or ambiguous, it can be non-trivial to decide what to retrieve and include. Include too little, and the model might not have the answer, include too much, and you risk overloading the context such that the model pays insufficient attention to each piece. There is also the phenomenon called "Lost-in-the-Middle" Effect" - models tend to pay a lot of attention to the beginning and end of the input, and can sometimes "lose" information in the middle of a very long context. This "lost in the middle" issue means that if you stuff, say, 50 passages into the context, the passages in the middle might have minimal impact on the generation. For RAG, this is a concern because typically we place user query at one end and maybe system instructions at the other, and retrieved texts in between - the ones in the middle of that sandwich might get under-attended. Researchers are actively investigating solutions: for example, chunking and hierarchical retrieval (first retrieve broad summaries, then drill down) to keep context focused, or using long-context models but still guiding their attention (via techniques like recency biasing or position-rescaling) to avoid middle-loss. Another aspect of the context bottleneck is irrelevant information dilution - if some retrieved text is slightly off-topic, it can confuse the model's answer. Too much context can be as bad as too little, because it increases the chance of the model picking up and following an irrelevant thread. This is one reason why larger context windows haven't eliminated RAG - as context grows, the difficulty of distilling the right information grows too. Giving a model a very long context doesn't guarantee the model uses it effectively - beyond a certain length, model outputs degrade.

    • Multi-Hop Reasoning vs. Complexity (Depth vs. Breadth): Basic RAG retrieves once per query. But what about questions that need reasoning through multiple hops? There's a design choice: do we try to have the model handle the multi-hop reasoning within one augmented prompt (by retrieving a lot of info at once), or do we explicitly break it into multiple retrieval-generations steps (iterative RAG)? Both approaches have trade-offs. Single-shot retrieval for multi-hop might require retrieving a broad set of documents hoping the answer is somewhere in there, which risks the context issues above and may still fail if the model doesn’t know how to combine them. Iterative retrieval, on the other hand, can guide the model through steps (retrieve -> partial answer -> use that to retrieve next, etc.), which often yields better results but at the cost of more moving parts and higher latency (multiple model calls). It also increases the risk of error propagation: if the first retrieval is wrong, the whole chain fails. There's a tension in how many hops of reasoning you support with retrieval. More hops = can solve more complex queries, but with diminishing returns and more complexity. For example, a recent multi-hop RAG benchmark (MultiHop-RAG) highlights the need for RAG systems to handle queries requiring multiple pieces of evidence. Systems like Hop RAG or others introduce graph-like retrieval to handle logic across documents. But implementing these adds significant complexity to the architecture (now you need a controller that decides the sequence of retrievals, maybe question decomposition modules, etc.). The design question is: When do you stop retrieving and trust the model to do the rest? If a query theoretically needs a 5-hop reasoning chain, is it worth engineering that, or do you revert to just giving the model whatever partial info you got and hoping it can bridge the gap? This is still an area of research and practical trial-and-error. The trade-off can also impact user experience, multi-hop processes might generate intermediate questions or outputs that need to be hidden or managed in a conversation. Conceptually, the tension is between completeness of reasoning and system efficiency/simplicity. Current systems often compromise by handling 2-hop or maybe 3-hop at most explicitly, and anything beyond that might be deemed out-of-scope (or handled by a specialised pipeline). As LLMs improve in reasoning, one might lean on the model to internally chain reasoning using the retrieved info, but if precision is critical, explicit multi-hop retrieval remains the more reliable (if slower) approach.

    • Semantic Recall vs. Exact Matching in Retrieval: RAG retrieval mostly relies on semantic similarity search (via embeddings), which has high recall for conceptually related information. However, semantic retrieval can sometimes retrieve text that is topically relevant but doesn't contain the exact fact needed, or it might miss a crucial document that doesn't share enough semantic features with the query (e.g., the query asks for a numerical value or a code snippet, which semantic search might not match well). Conversely, exact match (keyword) retrieval can pinpoint specific information (like a particular name or phrase) with high precision, but might miss relevant info phrased differently. Many RAG systems choose a hybrid approach to get the best of both. For instance, a system might first do a keyword filter to ensure certain keywords are present, then rerank results by embedding similarity. Or it might always perform both a vector search and a lexical search and merge the results (known as hybrid retrieval). Engineers need to consider the nature of the corpus - if the information is very structured or factual (like code, exact formulas, or proper nouns), a stronger exact-match component might be necessary. If it's more conceptual, pure semantic might work. The trade-off also affects robustness - semantic retrieval might retrieve text that seems relevant but is actually about a different context (false positives), potentially misleading the model, exact retrieval might give nothing if the wording is off (false negatives). All in all, deciding how to retrieve is a balancing act between broad semantic coverage and sharp exact matching.

    • Hallucination and Answer Integration: The LLM can still hallucinate or ignore retrieved info. Ensuring the model actually uses the retrieved facts and doesn't introduce contradictory or ungrounded statements is tough. There is a tension between a perfectly factual answer that might be a bit rigid (because it sticks religiously to the text) and a more fluent answer that might deviate.


    To wrap up these concerns - every strength in RAG comes with a corresponding challenge. Grounding answers improves accuracy but slows things down. Expanded context gives the model more to work with but can confuse it if not controlled. Multi-hop retrieval solves complex queries but makes the system more complex and slower. Semantic retrieval retrieves widely but might need exact matching to narrow down to precise detail. The key is that RAG design is about managing trade-offs - there is rarely a one-size-fits-all solution.
